content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

April 6, 1993


Margaret A. Sloan
City Attorney
26379 Fremont Road
Los Altos Hills, CA  94022

Re:  Your Request for Advice
     Our File No. A-93-095

Dear Ms. Sloan:

     This is in response to your request for advice on behalf of
Robert Johnson, Mayor pro tem of the Town of Los Altos Hills under
the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act.1/

QUESTION

     Does the "public generally" exception permit Mayor Johnson to
participate in a decision to set sewer rates affecting his real
property interests?

CONCLUSION

     The "public generally" exception applies to Mayor Johnson's
real property interests.  He may participate in the decision to
set sewer rates.

FACTS

     Robert Johnson was elected to the Los Altos City Council in
1988.  He and his wife own a personal residence within the Town of
Los Altos Hills.  His property is located in what is known as the
Palo Alto Sewer Basin.  Sewage from the properties that are
connected to sewers in the Palo Alto Sewer Basin flows through
pipes owned and maintained by the Town into pipes owned and
maintained by the City of Palo Alto and ultimately to the Palo
Alto sewage treatment plant.


1/     Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000 et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800  •  P.O. Box 807  •  Sacramento, CA  95812  •  (916) 322-5660

Advice File No. A-93-095
Page Two

Every year the City of Palo Alto determines the annual
service charge on each property within the Palo Alto Sewer Basin
for the privilege of maintaining each single residential
connection to the sanitary sewer facilities of the City of Palo
Alto. Shortly thereafter, the Town of Los Altos Hills sets an
additional service charge on each property within the Palo Alto
Sewer Basin for the privilege of maintaining each single
residential connection to the sanitary sewer facilities of the
Town. These service charges are levied against each parcel of
land, including the property owned by Mr. Johnson.

In a telephone conversation, you indicated that all single
family dwellings are assessed the same rate.

Los Altos Hills has a population of 7,512. All properties
within the Town, except for public facilities and a few stables,
are single family homesites. There are 2,662 homes in the Town,
of which 1,193 are connected to sewers. Five Hundred Eighty
homes with sewers are located in the Palo Alto Basin and 613 homes
are located in another sewer basin, where the sewage flows to the
City of Los Altos.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making,
participating in, or using their official position to influence a
governmental decision in which they know or have reason to know
they have a financial interest.

A public official has an interest within the meaning of
Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision
will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from the
effect on the public generally, on the official or a member of his
or her immediate family or on:

(b) Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

Section 87103(b)

Accordingly, Mr. Johnson may not make, participate in making,
or attempt to use his official position to influence a
governmental decision if the decision will have a reasonably
foreseeable2/ material financial effect, distinguishable from the
public generally, on his real property interests.

2/ Whether the financial consequences of a decision are
reasonably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made
depends on the facts of each particular case. An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
likelihood that it will occur. Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

Advice File No. A-93-095
Page Three

Material Financial Effect

Commission Regulation 18702.1 sets forth standards to
determine when a decision will have a material financial effect on
real property interests which are the subject of a decision. The
real property is deemed to be the subject of a decision, and
disqualification is required, if the decision involves the
imposition, repeal or modification of any taxes or fees assessed
or imposed on such property. (Regulation 18702.1(a)(3)(C).)

The decision before Mr. Johnson imposes a sewer fee on his
principal place of residence. Such decision has a direct material
financial effect on his real property interests. Disqualification
is required unless the decision is not distinguishable from the
effect on the public generally.

The "Public Generally" Exception

Public officials with financial interests that will be
materially affected by a decision may participate in the decision
if the effect on their property is not distinguishable from the
effect on the public generally. For the "public generally" exception to apply, a decision must affect the official's interests in
substantially the same manner as it would affect a significant
segment of the public. (Regulation 18703.)

The "public" consists of the entire jurisdiction of the
agency in question. (In re Owen (1976) 2 FPPC Ops. 77.) This is
so because all the residents of the jurisdiction are constituents
of the official. Thus, Mr. Johnson may participate in the
decision to assess a sewer fee if the effect on his financial
interests is substantially the same as the effect on a significant
segment of the population of the Town of Los Altos Hills.

We do not have population figures. The number of residences
in the Town is known, and because the sewer assessment is the same
on each single residential connection, the number of residential
units in the Town is an appropriate number to use. (In re Legan
(1985) 9 FPPC Ops. 13.)

You stated that of the 2,662 homes in the Town, 1,193 are
connected to sewers. Five Hundred Eighty homes with sewers are
located in the Palo Alto Basin and 613 homes are located in
another sewer basin. Approximately 22 percent of the total homes
in the Town are located in the Palo Alto Basin and subject to the
fee assessment.

The Commission has never adopted a strict arithmetic formula
for determining what constitutes a significant segment of the
public. However, in order to apply the "public generally"
exception, the affected population must be large and heterogeneous
in nature. (In re Ferraro (1978) 4 FPPC Ops. 62.) The population
affected appears to be heterogeneous in nature, and 22 percent of

Advice File No. A-93-095
Page Four

the dwelling units in the city represents a significant segment of
the public.4/ You indicated that all of the single family
dwellings within the basin would be assessed substantially the
same rate. Thus, the "public generally" exception would permit
Mr. Johnson to participate in the sewer fee assessment decision
since a significant portion of the population in the Town would be
affected in the same manner.5/ (See also Peck Advice Letter No.
I-92-215.)

I hope this has been helpful to you. If you need further
assistance, please feel free to contact the Commission's Legal
Division at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Jeff Marschner
General Counsel

By: Jeanette E. Turvill
Political Reform Consultant
Legal Division

JM/JET/jt


4/ The "public generally" exception is an exception to the
general rules requiring disqualification on the basis of an
official's economic interests. It is a fundamental rule of
statutory construction that an exception to a general rule is to
be narrowly construed. (Julius Goldman's Inc. City v. Air
Pollution Control Dept. of Ventura County (1981) 116 Cal.App.3d
741.) As such, the narrow interpretation of the exception is
preferred.

5/ The Commission will be considering amendment of its existing
regulation concerning "public generally" (Regulation 18703) at its
March 1993 meeting. As a result, our analysis of the "public
generally" issue may change and it could affect our future advice
on this issue.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does the “public generally” exception permit Mayor Johnson to
participate in a decision to set sewer rates affecting his real
property interests?

CONCLUSION: The “public generally” exception applies to Mayor Johnson’s
real property interests.
He may participate in the decision to
set sewer rates.
